APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses automatic keyphrase extraction, a core NLP/information extraction problem directly relevant to top-tier NLP venues. It uses standard, venue-appropriate methodology (graph-based PageRank variant) and evaluation practices (MRR, Precision/Recall/F1, performance@k) on established scholarly datasets and compares to canonical baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004; Hulth 2003; etc.). The audience and positioning are appropriate for an NLP/IR community interested in unsupervised methods for scholarly document processing. | B(5): This paper addresses automatic keyphrase extraction, a core NLP/information-extraction task, using an unsupervised PositionRank variant that is directly relevant to top NLP/ML audiences. It builds a word co-occurrence graph and a position-biased PageRank, compares against well-known baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau; Hulth; Wan & Xiao; Hasan & Ng), and evaluates on standard scholarly datasets (CiteSeerX KDD/WWW, Nguyen & Kan) with appropriate metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, MRR). The methods and evaluation protocol match expectations for a top-tier venue and the topic is squarely within the conference scope.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses automatic keyphrase extraction, a core NLP/information extraction problem directly relevant to top-tier NLP venues. It uses standard, venue-appropriate methodology (graph-based PageRank variant) and evaluation practices (MRR, Precision/Recall/F1, performance@k) on established scholarly datasets and compares to canonical baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004; Hulth 2003; etc.). The audience and positioning are appropriate for an NLP/IR community interested in unsupervised methods for scholarly document processing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses automatic keyphrase extraction, a core NLP/information-extraction task, using an unsupervised PositionRank variant that is directly relevant to top NLP/ML audiences. It builds a word co-occurrence graph and a position-biased PageRank, compares against well-known baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau; Hulth; Wan & Xiao; Hasan & Ng), and evaluates on standard scholarly datasets (CiteSeerX KDD/WWW, Nguyen & Kan) with appropriate metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, MRR). The methods and evaluation protocol match expectations for a top-tier venue and the topic is squarely within the conference scope.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents substantive experimental comparisons across three established datasets and multiple strong baselines (PageRank variants, Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao, Hulth, Hasan & Ng) using standard metrics (MRR, precision, recall, F1, @k) and reports substantial gains. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported to isolate the contribution of position-bias versus other design choices, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, making it adequate but missing important analyses needed for thorough validation. | B(3): The paper evaluates PositionRank on multiple scholarly datasets (two CiteSeerX collections and Nguyen & Kan), compares against several strong baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao, Hulth, Hasan & Ng) and reports multiple metrics (MRR, precision, recall, F1), which provides reasonable experimental support for the main claims. However, the summary indicates no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of window size, phrase-length choices, or the position-bias formulation) and no discussion of failure cases or limitations. Reproducibility is limited because hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned. Given solid baseline comparisons but missing key analyses and reproducibility details, the work is adequately evaluated but not fully comprehensive.

Reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code, making it adequate but missing important analyses needed for thorough validation.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from problem to method to experiments, and its main contributions (PositionRank, position-biased PageRank, and reported improvements) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented coherently — the graph construction, biased PageRank formulation, and candidate phrase regex ((adjective)*(noun)+ up to trigrams) are described plainly — and the writing quality is noted as good with adequate related work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported, no limitations or code availability are mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and practical clarity. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and the main contributions (PositionRank and its use of word-position information) are stated early and followed by a clear methodology and experiments across multiple datasets. Notation and method description appear consistent and readable (e.g., graph construction, regex for candidate phrases, scoring), and the writing quality is reported as good with adequate related work coverage. Minor presentation issues include lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of code availability or whether figures/tables are used effectively, which slightly reduces reproducibility and completeness of presentation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments against appropriate baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao, Hulth, Hasan & Ng) on multiple datasets (CiteSeerX KDD/WWW, Nguyen & Kan) with reported gains (up to 29.09%). The proposed algorithm (position-biased PageRank on a word co-occurrence graph, candidate extraction via (adjective)*(noun)+ trigrams) is technically plausible and requires no complex proofs. However, methodological reporting is incomplete: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, and no statistical significance testing is described, which are minor but important reproducibility and rigor gaps. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: PositionRank is evaluated against reasonable baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao, Hulth, Hasan & Ng) on three scholarly-paper datasets with standard metrics (MRR, precision, recall, F1) and reports improvements up to 29.09%. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify and the algorithmic description (position-biased PageRank on a noun/adjective co-occurrence graph with (adjective)*(noun)+ trigram candidates) is internally consistent. However, experimental reporting omits hyperparameter details, statistical significance tests, and code availability, and the candidate-selection constraint (max trigrams, POS filter) is an implicit assumption that could affect generality. Overall the technical claims are well supported but these missing experimental details are minor gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments against appropriate baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau, Wan & Xiao, Hulth, Hasan & Ng) on multiple datasets (CiteSeerX KDD/WWW, Nguyen & Kan) with reported gains (up to 29.09%). The proposed algorithm (position-biased PageRank on a word co-occurrence graph, candidate extraction via (adjective)*(noun)+ trigrams) is technically plausible and requires no complex proofs. However, methodological reporting is incomplete: hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, and no statistical significance testing is described, which are minor but important reproducibility and rigor gaps.

There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify and the algorithmic description (position-biased PageRank on a noun/adjective co-occurrence graph with (adjective)*(noun)+ trigram candidates) is internally consistent. However, experimental reporting omits hyperparameter details, statistical significance tests, and code availability, and the candidate-selection constraint (max trigrams, POS filter) is an implicit assumption that could affect generality. Overall the technical claims are well supported but these missing experimental details are minor gaps.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a clear, focused novelty by incorporating all occurrence positions of words into a position-biased PageRank for keyphrase extraction and combining this with a noun/adjective co-occurrence graph and simple candidate phrase regex; this is a non-trivial extension of prior PageRank-based methods (e.g., Mihalcea and Tarau 2004) and is shown to improve performance against strong baselines. However, keyphrase extraction is a well-studied problem and the contribution is principally an incremental modification of existing graph-ranking approaches rather than a fundamentally new formulation or paradigm, so the novelty is moderate rather than high. | B(3): The paper applies a position-biased PageRank by incorporating all occurrence positions of words into the ranking, which is a clear methodological tweak to existing graph-based keyphrase extraction (e.g., PageRank-style methods). The problem (keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents) is well-studied, and the core idea is an incremental but non-trivial extension rather than a wholly new paradigm. The authors distinguish their contribution from prior PageRank approaches and demonstrate empirical gains on established datasets, so the work is moderately novel but not a radical departure from prior methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear, focused novelty by incorporating all occurrence positions of words into a position-biased PageRank for keyphrase extraction and combining this with a noun/adjective co-occurrence graph and simple candidate phrase regex; this is a non-trivial extension of prior PageRank-based methods (e.g., Mihalcea and Tarau 2004) and is shown to improve performance against strong baselines. However, keyphrase extraction is a well-studied problem and the contribution is principally an incremental modification of existing graph-ranking approaches rather than a fundamentally new formulation or paradigm, so the novelty is moderate rather than high.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies a position-biased PageRank by incorporating all occurrence positions of words into the ranking, which is a clear methodological tweak to existing graph-based keyphrase extraction (e.g., PageRank-style methods). The problem (keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents) is well-studied, and the core idea is an incremental but non-trivial extension rather than a wholly new paradigm. The authors distinguish their contribution from prior PageRank approaches and demonstrate empirical gains on established datasets, so the work is moderately novel but not a radical departure from prior methods.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets the important task of keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents and does so with an unsupervised method (PositionRank) that is broadly applicable. Reported improvements up to 29.09% over strong baselines across multiple datasets (CiteSeerX KDD, CiteSeerX WWW, Nguyen & Kan) suggest the results would influence practitioner choices and evaluation baselines. The core idea—incorporating all occurrence positions into a biased PageRank on a word co-occurrence graph—is a generally useful technique that can spawn follow-up work and extensions. While primarily within NLP/IR, the approach has clear downstream utility for scholarly search, summarization, and metadata extraction. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—automatic keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents—and proposes PositionRank, an unsupervised, position-biased PageRank that aggregates all occurrence positions of words. It reports substantial improvements (up to 29.09%) over strong baselines (including prior PageRank variants and other established methods) on multiple standard datasets (CiteSeerX KDD and WWW, Nguyen and Kan), suggesting the results could change practitioner choices toward position-aware graph methods. As an unsupervised approach with consistent gains, it opens clear follow-up directions for position-informed ranking and is likely to be built upon, though its focus on scholarly texts and specific candidate-phrase heuristics may constrain immediate broader applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets the important task of keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents and does so with an unsupervised method (PositionRank) that is broadly applicable. Reported improvements up to 29.09% over strong baselines across multiple datasets (CiteSeerX KDD, CiteSeerX WWW, Nguyen & Kan) suggest the results would influence practitioner choices and evaluation baselines. The core idea—incorporating all occurrence positions into a biased PageRank on a word co-occurrence graph—is a generally useful technique that can spawn follow-up work and extensions. While primarily within NLP/IR, the approach has clear downstream utility for scholarly search, summarization, and metadata extraction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—automatic keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents—and proposes PositionRank, an unsupervised, position-biased PageRank that aggregates all occurrence positions of words. It reports substantial improvements (up to 29.09%) over strong baselines (including prior PageRank variants and other established methods) on multiple standard datasets (CiteSeerX KDD and WWW, Nguyen and Kan), suggesting the results could change practitioner choices toward position-aware graph methods. As an unsupervised approach with consistent gains, it opens clear follow-up directions for position-informed ranking and is likely to be built upon, though its focus on scholarly texts and specific candidate-phrase heuristics may constrain immediate broader applicability.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and strong baselines (PageRank variants, Mihalcea & Tarau 2004, Wan & Xiao 2008, Hulth 2003, Hasan & Ng 2014) using standard datasets (CiteSeerX KDD/WWW, Nguyen & Kan) and common metrics (MRR, precision/recall/F1). However, hyperparameters and code are not reported and the paper states no limitations or whether prior methods sometimes perform comparably, which raises transparency/fairness concerns. Overall the comparisons are good with only minor omissions, so I rate it a 4. | B(4): The paper compares against established baselines (Mihalcea & Tarau 2004; Hulth 2003; Wan & Xiao 2008; Hasan & Ng 2014) on standard datasets (CiteSeerX KDD/WWW, Nguyen & Kan) and reports common metrics (MRR, Precision, Recall, F1), which indicates broadly appropriate baselines and evaluation. However, hyperparameters are not reported and code is not mentioned, and the summary notes no stated limitations or discussion of when prior methods might perform comparably, which weakens fairness/diagnostic transparency. Overall the comparisons are good but have minor omissions in reporting and discussion, so I score it a 4.

main_claims: Proposes PositionRank, an unsupervised model for keyphrase extraction from scholarly documents.
PositionRank incorporates information from all positions of a word’s occurrences into a biased PageRank.
PositionRank obtains substantial improvements over PageRank models that do not use word-position information and over other strong baselines (improvements up to 29.09% reported).
PositionRank builds a word-level undirected co-occurrence graph (using nouns and adjectives) and forms candidate noun phrases matching the regex (adjective)*(noun)+ up to trigrams.
_summary.methodology: Build an undirected word co-occurrence graph from document tokens (keeping nouns and adjectives, edges for co-occurrence within a window w), run a position-biased PageRank that incorporates all occurrence positions of words, form candidate phrases by concatenating contiguous candidate words matching (adjective)*(noun)+ up to length three, and score phrases by summing their constituent word scores.
datasets: CiteSeerX KDD dataset (from Gollapalli and Caragea, 2014)
CiteSeerX WWW dataset (from Gollapalli and Caragea, 2014)
Nguyen and Kan (2007) dataset (research papers from various disciplines)
baselines: PageRank models that do not take into account word positions
_summary.topic_area: NLP - keyphrase extraction / information extraction from scholarly documents
